speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is July the 21st, 1995. The survivor's name is Maria Scheffer. The interviewer's name is Barbara Linz. And the interview will be conducted in English, in Sydney, Australia. OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Barbara Linz-- L-I-N-Z. It's July the 21st, 1995. And I'm conducting an interview with Maria Scheffer in Sydney, Australia, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name and how you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Maria Scheffer. And it's S-C-H-E-F-F-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was you name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was actually Maria Scheffer, because it's my maiden name. I've kept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I've got a middle name, Klara. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a Hungarian nickname, which sounds Picu. That's P-I-C-U, and it means 'a small one.' Do you want to know how I got rid of it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was three, I thought, I'm not small anymore. I don't want anybody calling me Picu. So I said, next person that calls me Picu gets a kick in the shin. And I did it! [LAUGHS] Within weeks, I was Maria. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 30th of November, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be 65 in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pecs, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we begin the interview by you telling me about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, certainly. My-- I-- I come from-- from a very loving, very normal, very average family. My parents were both medical practitioners. They were doctors. And they were-- especially, my father was attached to a university in a small town, Pecs, which is an old, old town in Hungary. And so he was attached to a university, apart from having a-- a private practice as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we were brought up in a small, country town. Old town, I think it's about 1,000 years old, with the Turkish minarets and-- and catacombs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I had two sisters. One is [? Zsoka. ?] And she's a year older. And she's here, thank God. The other was four and a half years younger than I. And her name was Zsuzsa. And, unfortunately, she's not here. She died in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was you father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leslie, Laszlo, Dr. Laszlo Scheffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth, Elizabeth-- Dr. Elizabeth Csillag. That's a rather difficult name. And so we were-- we were-- actually, I-- I-- I really should talk about the fact that I'm Christian-born. Because what had happened was, my father was, supposedly, half-Jewish only. His father was supposedly Jewish. And his mother was not. She was-- she was a half-Russian, half-Italian. And my mother, on the other hand, was of entirely Jewish descent. But my father was baptized as a young child. I-- I remember-- well, I mean, I don't remember it, but I'm told that he was about five. My sister thinks that he was a little bit older. Not that it matters one way or another. But, anyway, he was a child. And my mother was somewhere around 15 or 16, I think, when she was. But she-- she became a Catholic of her own [BACKGROUND NOISE] choosing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it-- honestly, I feel that it had-- it was a religious-- a religious conversion. Why she chose-- I don't-- I don't-- I don't know why. You see, when-- when I was a child, we didn't talk about it, because, unfortunately, they didn't tell us that we're-- we've got Jewish background. So, obviously, that didn't then-- [LAUGHS] you know, it couldn't have come up. And then, later on, after the war, it was a subject that we-- not-- not just her conversion, but the whole thing, she couldn't cope. See, my father, of course, was killed in Auschwitz, as well. And for my mother, this was something that we could never, ever again touch. We couldn't talk about it. We never talked about my father. We never talked about my sister. So you couldn't ask, why did you do such and such or-- because she couldn't handle anything. She was like a sphinx.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I, unfortunately, can't give you a-- a really proper answer, because I don't know. But I do believe that she had some sort of religious calling. Oh, that sounds as if she became a nun. She didn't. [LAUGHS] But she-- she had some sort of affinity, I guess, with-- with the Catholic religion. Did her parents also convert or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, her-- her mother was already dead. Actually, her-- her mother died in 1914. And he-- she actually committed suicide, because she was very, very ill. She had a thyroid disorder. And, in those days, of course, that couldn't be helped. And she knew what she was going into. And she knew what the family had to put up with in the long run. And she wasn't going to ruin her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she actually committed suicide, which was a terrible thing, but it was a very courageous thing from her point of view. But, anyway, so she was dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandfather, never. My grandfather, never. I-- To the best of my knowledge-- I could stand corrected-- I don't think he was religious. But he was a very proud man. And he would not have changed his religion for anything. No, he was a Jew. And that was it. He was a very interesting man, actually. He was a-- well, he was quite a brilliant man. He was a solicitor, very, very successful solicitor, a very good writer, excellent writer, a very good painter and cartoonist, a practical joker, a duel player.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or is that what they call it, a duel-- dueler [LAUGHS]? I think he-- he had something like [LAUGHS] 50 duels in his life. So anyway, he was quite a character. So, anyhow, the main thing is that, no, he didn't ever convert. Did they live in Pecs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they-- they came from-- from the southern part of Hungary, which became-- became, after the war, was-- was attached to Yugoslavia-- after the First World War, attached to Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had to get out, because my grandfather was a great, great Hungarian. You know, his home, his country, his all that. And so he-- he-- he was literally thrown out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was given-- well, offered a very high position in-- in his field, of course. And-- and he said, I have sworn to the Hungarian law not to the Yugoslav law. So they said, well then, goodbye. So they threw him out. And they had to go up to Budapest, then. And so my mother and my aunt-- Aunt Vera, who is dead-- they-- they both went to university. And they were virtually hungry for the university years, because there was no-- no one to-- you know, to-- to keep them while they were studying. So it was very hard times. But they did it. And that's the main thing, they did it. So that's-- that's-- that's the background. On my mother's-- or my-- sorry, about my-- my father, on my father's side, there was certainly no hint of any-- any religious Jewishness at all, nothing, nothing. My grandfather, as much as I remember him, I've never, ever heard him touch on the subject. And I-- I heard a lot of stories about my grandmother, because that was interesting. Because her mother was a Russian aristocrat, actually. And she was a concert pianist. But she was-- she-- they wouldn't let her actually concertise, because that wasn't done. [LAUGHS] But-- and-- and then she married an-- an Italian, who was also a doctor. And they lived in the most incredible places in the world. So that was a-- a fascinating story. So I heard about that. But I didn't hear much about my grandfather, because he was just a-- he was a veterinary surgeon. And it was a normal [LAUGHS] Hungarian family and nothing very romantic. So that's-- that-- that was-- I had lots cousins. None-- excepting one, none-- none of the others were touched by anything, because they, the-- [SIGHS] the men and the women, married out of-- out of the-- the Jewish religion entirely. And if you were half-Jewish and married a non-Jew, then you weren't you know, you-- you weren't considered to be Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- one, as I said, one cousin was, and she died. She died. She-- she-- she died in Auschwitz and so did her mother. So they're the only-- But, then, there were some aunts of my father's who-- who-- who were taken. Aunt, one aunt of my father's was taken. Because of being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, because of being Jewish. So that's-- that's how-- but whether they were actually Jewish or not, I don't know, because they didn't live in Pecs. But I know that they were, because she married a Jew. So, of course, they were taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of medicine did your father practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was what one-- I guess one would call a-- a physician, an internalist. But he specialized in thyroid. Incredible coincidence, you know, my grandmother having died of it. But that was-- that's was-- that's what was his specialty. And he-- he did a lot of research. He-- he-- he was-- by the time he died, he was a-- an accepted and recognized world authority on thyroid. And even-- even after the war, we-- we had-- we got a-- one of the medical papers-- medical journals from America was actually quoting him. My mother, my God, when she saw that. You know, one of his books, quoted from one of his books. And-- and also, the-- she-- he-- Oxford University was actually looking for him. They tried to locate him after the war. But, of course, we don't know where he-- he died. Or well, we know he died in Auschwitz. But we don't know exactly when.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were sent to nuns, inevitably, of course. And we were-- all three of us were good at school. And we-- we had a great time at school. We played a lot of sport. There was an awful lot of music in our house. And I was-- I-- I was going to be or, I guess, I was going to try to be a pianist. I don't think I was talented enough, mind you. But at that stage, that-- you know, we thought that I might be. And my sister wanted to be a doctor. But no, I-- I thought I was going to be a pianist. And so we played-- we played a lot of music, a lot of sport. We had lots of friends. And just like other normal-- normal children, just an absolutely normal life. Although, it wasn't quite as normal as we thought, but we didn't know. There were certain things that we were not allowed to-- to do. For instance, both my sister and I were very good swimmers. And we were not allowed to go into competition.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we could never understand, why can't we go into competition? But, of course, we couldn't, because we weren't allowed. But we didn't know that, because my parents didn't tell us that we are of Jewish descent. So we just couldn't understand why our parents are so old-fashioned and backward that they won't let us-- [LAUGHS] won't let us swim. Well, sorry, they let us swim, but they didn't let us-- to-- they didn't let us compete. So-- so, there-- there were these things. And there were, in school, in-- in my class, there were one or two girls who were of high-ranking soldiers' daughters or a politician. There was one girl, whose father was a politician. Need I say, right-wing, of course? And I was never allowed to invite them to my parties. And I could never-- never understand why. Now, why can't I? They were nice girls. Why can't I invite them? And my mother-- my mother always said, no, you're not going to invite such and such.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I couldn't invite them, because they would have never come. But my mother didn't want me to-- and I guess it would have been the thing for my sister. I don't know. I imagine it would have been the same for my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that stuck in my mind. And I wondered, why on Earth can't I invite? And, then, of course, I thought, well, obviously, I'm not invited there, because I don't invite them. So you know, I want to invite them, because I want to go there, because when the others were having birthday parties. So, anyway. So that was peculiar, but we didn't know. Were you religious Catholics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were. We were religious Catholics. Well, having gone to nuns, you know? And yes, we were. We were very practicing, religious Catholics. We-- we had-- we had-- we had to go to, naturally, church every Sunday. That was with school. I mean you couldn't not. Mind you, we-- we wouldn't have not, because that would've been a terrible sin. So-- [LAUGHS] so we never ate meat on Friday, ever, never. On a-- on Good Friday, we-- we were virtually hungry all day. [LAUGHS] They wouldn't give us anything to eat, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yeah, we were truly a-- a-- a praying family. And-- and often-- my father never came to church with us. But I often saw him. You know, he'd be standing at another-- another-- when I say, often, not-- not every week. I mean, they probably, my-- with my mother, they went elsewhere and to another church. But I did. A great number of times, I saw him in church. And-- Was there antisemitism in the school or in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was antisemitism, but this-- this is a very peculiar-- I-- I have very mixed memories with this, because, for a long time, I wasn't at the receiving end. Although, there were two Jewish girls in-- in my class, and we were very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they weren't-- so there was antisemitism, but it wasn't turned on these two girls. They were both-- mind you, I don't know exactly where they were invited. They were certainly invited to our place. But whether they were as much in with the others, I'm afraid I wouldn't like to say, because I just can't remember. But the antisemitism wasn't such, at that stage, that one would have said, what's happening here? No. Do you know what was-- what-- what did happen to us, and especially to me, because I had a very Jewish nose, which is now-- well, has been operated. You know, it's been sliced a long time ago. [LAUGHS] Anyway, we-- in the street, we'd be called dirty Jews, filthy Jews, stinking Jews, you know whatever. And often, we'd say to my mother, what on Earth are these people talking about? And she said, you-- you have-- you look Italian, which was true, my-- especially my sister looked very Italian-ish, because my father looked Italian-- well, half-Italian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, we-- we-- we believed anything we were told. So we believed that we are getting these-- these 'dirty Jews,' because-- because we looked Italian, and these silly people can't tell the difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, certainly, there was antisemitism. There was great antisemitism in Hungary. It was-- I always-- I always believe and will always believe that antisemitism in Hungary virtually had nothing to do with Hitler, because it was there before. [LAUGHS] Yeah, well, I-- I can't say before anyone heard of Hitler. Obviously, that would be silly. But it was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was 'numerus clausus.' That means 5% of Jews were allowed into the universities since 1920. Or-- or I might stand corrected, and it doesn't matter whether it is '20 or '25. But that's why all the Jewish boys were sent out of the country to universities. Because-- because they just couldn't go to universities. And that was so many years before the war. And then, of course, 'numerus-- numerus nullus' came in, in the second part of the '30s, I think that was. So there was very strong antisemitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that, 'numerus nullus?'

speaker: interviewee
text: That was no one, that-- that no more Jews in the universities at all. You know, they were just banned from then on. So no Jew could go to universities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening to the Jews in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not. This is-- I'm now talking about my sisters and I. Now, I'm not talking about my parents. I have no idea. But-- but very definitely, absolutely no. We didn't. See, when the war started, I was nine years old. And that is a bit too young, you know, to-- to be-- to be concerned with this. And my parents, rightly or wrongly-- I believe very wrongly, but I can understand them-- kept us away from the problem, completely. They just didn't want us. And, indeed, this is something that a lot of people do today. You know, rather, the first generation to the second generation, and they grow up not knowing because-- of the-- the background, because the parents say, we cannot-- we cannot upset the children. I don't agree with that, myself. Mind you, I have no children, so I've got no right to speak. But I don't agree with it. However, this is what a lot of them do. If they feel-- you know, they-- they feel that they need to shelter the children from this dreadful hurt. But, of course, I believe that you can't, because, sooner or later, you identify. You just-- you know, it can't be. I can see that from my sister's children. My younger nephew, he's so interested. He's so deeply, deeply in-- not involved in-- I don't want to tell a lie-- not involved in a Jewish life, because that's certainly not true. But he's involved in the memories. And he keeps saying, the only people that he's at ease with are other Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- what-- what was the reaction to the anti-Jewish laws that were being passed, did you-- from your parents or your friends or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I'm very sorry. I'm-- I'm so-- [LAUGHS] so ignorant on this. But as I said, I didn't know there were-- I can't remember that they were-- that I knew that there were actual Jewish laws. I didn't know. I really didn't know. But I guess-- I guess the truth is I was young. I mean at 9 and 10, you don't. You know, you live your life. And you go to your children's parties. And you play the piano. And you do acrobatics. And you dance, and, you know, all those things as children do. And I-- I just can't-- I cannot remember. I cannot. Maybe my sister does. I don't. I just don't. But I found out, then, one day. I sure found out. Well, I mean-- I mean, about my-- about our background. And that was at school. I-- I had a pretty good school record. And I was captain of-- of the class. Actually, all, sort of all-- when I say, always, I mean, from the time when we had captains. And then, and-- and there was a-- there was some sort of national day. I've reconstructed that in the last few days, actually. There was a national day. And I was given a rather prominent role to play in whatever was going on. And when all that was over, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my teacher, head teacher, said a few words to the class about me, which was awfully nice of her. And next day, I was asked out from class and taken into a little room in the gymnasium. And there was the mother of one of my classmates, who was obviously beside herself, livid. And she was screaming. And she was screaming at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The moment I went in, she was screaming at me, 'dirty Jew.' How-- and she was saying to my teacher, 'how dare you put the--' not child of a dirty Jew. I just can't think of an-- a good equivalent word. A brat, maybe, but even worse. 'The brat of a dirty Jew above my Aryan daughter?' 'How dare you keep her as exam--' oh, whatever it was, you know-- to-- to-- to hold her as an example or whatever? So.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this went on. This went on for God knows how long. And I kept-- I-- I-- I wanted to interrupt and say, but, you know, you're wrong. You're wrong. But, of course, I didn't have a chance. Anyhow, I started crying, naturally. And she was saying to my teacher that, if I'm not removed as captain and if I'm ever given another role as a Hungarian, the police will hear about it. Anyway, so, oh, of course, I was crying. And I went home and told my mother that this dreadful thing has happened. And I said, 'Please go straight in there and put this right.' And so my mother said, 'Well, sit down. I've got something to tell you.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I went back-- I-- I stayed away from school for about three days. And then, when I went back, it was all changed. No, I wasn't school captain anymore. And I-- I had-- up till then, I had all, you know, top marks. I had A's in everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from then on, they always, on something, they gave me a-- a B, just so that it can't be said that I was a straight-A. Because straight A's were, you know, special.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, anyway, but then, this didn't last an awful long time, because the Germans came in within the year. So I was then 12 when this happened. And within a year, the Germans marched in. And so [LAUGHS] the school days were over anyhow. But I do remember that, from then on, after this happened, I had lost all confidence. And the-- the whole world had changed. You know, it's-- it-- it just-- it wasn't me anymore. And-- and I really had to try and work out, well, exactly who am I? Where do I belong? I didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a while to-- to-- to work that out, but I did. I did. And it didn't-- it didn't cause me-- the fact that I found out that I was Jewish, it was a shock, of course, when I was told. But it didn't cause me a-- it wasn't a-- a tragedy. You know, it wasn't-- it didn't-- I got used to the idea in no time at all. And that was how it was, and that was fine. But I knew someone-- we knew someone who, a-- a somewhat similar story, and he committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he found out that he was a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you didn't mind being a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. I didn't. I didn't. It was strange that this was how things were. But both my sister and I-- I'm not talking about my little sister, because she was far too young. But, no, we-- we somehow-- you know, yeah, we accepted it. I can't remember, after the initial shock, to have been-- that it-- it would've been a huge problem, you know, a psychological problem. I really don't remember that it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, anyway. [SIGHS] And then, of course, [SIGHS] one day, the 19th of March in 1944, Germans marched in. And that was-- I'm repeating myself to the extent that I've said this often before. But I really mean it, honestly and absolutely, that that hour-- from that hour, when the Germans came in, it was zero hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from then on, never again has life been anything what it had been before. You know, it's like an-- another time, another life, another planet, another everything. It's just changed. Never again. Never again has it been the same from that hour onwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a Sunday morning. And my sisters and I were going, as usual, you know, to mass. And the only thing that was slightly strange, that there were planes flying fairly low. And we-- we noticed that. But-- yeah, we remarked on it. You know, what are all these planes up there? Anyway, then we went to mass. And when we came out, honestly, without actually seeing anything, the atmosphere, there was something so different. And we couldn't-- we didn't know, what is it? What's happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we realized that what we're hearing, from a certain distance, was thump, thump, thump, Germans marching. You know, the-- the-- the columns was-- were marching somewhere down, I don't know how-- how many streets down, wherever. But we could hear this thump, thump, thump. And it was-- it was frightening. It was-- well, it was so strange, of course. And then slowly, the-- we started hearing the motorized transport. It-- they must have, I guess, just reached the center of town or, you know, our area. Because there-- there was this-- this incredible changed atmosphere. And so we-- we went-- we went-- went home, of course. There was my father and my mother. Both of them are like they were different people. They were white as-- as a sheet. And they told us that we've got to face very difficult times, because the Germans have marched in. And it means, to us-- not that they would have pointed out what it actually meant to us. I don't think they knew that. Well, in fact, I'm sure they didn't. Because my father was offered a chair in-- in one of the US universities in 193-- '39, 1940, and he didn't accept it. Because he said he can't-- he can't do this to his family and, you know, to-- to uproot everyone. And, also, because he truly believed that, no matter what, it-- nothing's going to happen to his family because-- because of him. So they couldn't have known. They must've known something. But they couldn't have known the extent. I don't think they would have heard about Auschwitz, myself. Again, I can't ask-- couldn't have asked my mother later, because she wouldn't have talked about it. But I don't think they would have known about Auschwitz. They certainly knew what's happening in Germany, obviously. There are the terrible things there. But I don't think that they-- they would have known about the extermination camps. Anyway, I'm-- I'm going off the-- going off the line. We were, from then on, my father stopped us from even going near the window. His idea was that, if you-- if you stay invisible, maybe we can. So he went-- he went along to my school and tried-- tried to-- to get the nuns to hide us. And the nuns said, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went to the bishop. And there were catacombs in my town. They were-- Pecs is so old, there were catacombs everywhere. And my father asked the bishop to at least-- at least hide my young sister in the catacombs. No, they wouldn't give any help, whatsoever. So, of course, there was no more school, need I say. The stars came in very, very quickly. I can't say exactly when, but, you know, if-- if it-- if it-- if I say a week, I don't think I'm much out-- straightaway.

speaker: interviewee
text: People immediately started disappearing, because the dear Hungarians flooded the Gestapo with letters. I do remember hearing that nowhere did they get the flood of letters as they got in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: To belong to the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. To-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: About Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To give people to-- to put people in. You know, such and such is a Jew. Such and such is a Social-- Social Democrat. Such and such is a Communist. Such and such is this. They absolutely flooded. So people started to disappear immediately. And my-- my parents, both my mother and father were actually asked into the headquarters, the Hungarian headquarters, police headquarters. And they were-- they were grilled, not physically but verbally grilled for a day wanting-- They were trying to find out, you know, where money was or-- not that we-- we-- we certainly weren't-- weren't in that class. You know, because doctors, they-- obviously, they're not businesspeople. They-- they earn well, but there is no question of-- of a-- a lot of money hidden away somewhere. Anyway, they believed them, apparently. And both came home that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, of course, it was a horrible shock. And the three of us were home together. We weren't allowed to leave the-- the house-- the-- the-- the flat. And it was terrible. We were so afraid. We didn't know whether we'd see them again. And if we don't see them again, then what happens to us? Anyhow, we did. They came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my parents decided that they'll try and hide us in the country. So they sent us, first, to a place quite near Pecs where we had very good friends. Well, we were hardly there for a few days, and, of course, someone went to the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there are three people who don't belong here. Check them out. Or they may have just said, there's three Jews, who knows? And so the police came and took us back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my parents decided to try another place a bit further away from Pecs. Exactly the same thing happened in no time at all. So we were back. And-- and I think they were probably told not to-- not to leave. Well, by then, it was getting very difficult, anyway. And we weren't allowed to leave the flat. My father was allowed to leave the flat, so he-- whatever food we could get, which wasn't much, because you had-- you had-- you-- you were a ration-- ration cards. And you could-- you couldn't-- being Jewish, you couldn't get milk, you couldn't get butter, you couldn't get meat. You  could hardly get anything. But we did, in fact. There was a neighbor, and to this day, I think I know who it may have been, but I don't really know. Every night, there was a tray outside our door with food, the dinner for-- for the lot of us. Which was a very brave thing to do, because they could've-- they could've paid for it very-- whoever it was, God bless them. But we-- so anyway, we were-- so we were-- we were given food by-- by others. Otherwise, we would have been unbelievably hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break for the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one of the interview with Maria Scheffer. It's the 21st of July, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.
